runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 6 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
CBO microsimulation model documentation - labor supply elasticities by income group
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation parameterizations
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners typically have low but positive substitution elasticities. Decile 6 represents middle-income workers who are strongly attached to the labor force, so intensive-margin responses are modest. The literature on taxable income elasticities for primary earners clusters around 0.1–0.3, with microsimulation models often calibrating substitution elasticities in this range. Middle deciles tend to have slightly lower elasticities than top or bottom deciles. I center my estimate at 0.20, with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and estimation strategies.

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 6) typically have modest labor supply substitution elasticities. The literature on taxable income elasticities and labor supply for prime-age primary earners generally centers around 0.1–0.3, with CBO and similar microsimulation models often using values near 0.2 for middle-income workers. Decile 6 is solidly middle-income, where workers face moderate effective marginal tax rates and have limited scope for adjustment compared to higher deciles. The bounded relative-change framing in microsimulation models often yields elasticities calibrated in this range. I center my estimate at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 6 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber & Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
CBO microsimulation labor supply parameters (various years)
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 6) typically have relatively low but positive substitution elasticities. The broad labor supply literature for prime-age primary earners centers around 0.1–0.3 for compensated/substitution elasticities. In microsimulation contexts (e.g., CBO, TAXSIM-based models), the parameters used for primary earners in middle deciles tend to be modest—often in the 0.15–0.25 range—reflecting that these workers are largely on interior solutions with limited extensive-margin response. Decile 6 is solidly middle-income, where behavioral responses to marginal tax rate changes are moderate. I center my estimate at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and estimation strategies.

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) microsimulation substitution elasticity assumptions

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 6), substitution elasticities in U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations are typically modest. The literature on taxable income elasticities (Gruber & Saez, Saez et al.) generally finds compensated elasticities for middle-income primary earners in the range of 0.1–0.4, with central estimates around 0.2–0.25. CBO and similar microsimulation models often calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in this range for middle deciles, which are lower than those for secondary earners and lower than those at the tails of the distribution. Decile 6 is solidly middle-income, where labor supply responses to marginal wage changes are relatively inelastic. I center my estimate at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and identification strategies.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 6) generally have relatively inelastic labor supply. runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 6) are typically modeled with modest substitution elasticities. The labor supply literature consistently finds that prime-age primary earners have relatively low wage elasticities of labor supply, generally in the range of 0.1–0.4. In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations (e.g., CBO-style models), substitution elasticities for primary earners in this range are commonly calibrated around 0.15–0.25. I center my estimate at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across different model calibrations and definitions of the bounded wage change.

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners (typically men or main household earners) in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 6), substitution elasticities used in U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations tend to be modest. The broad labor supply literature places compensated (substitution) elasticities for prime-age men around 0.1–0.3, with somewhat higher values when considering hours and participation margins together. Decile 6 represents a solidly middle-income group where labor force attachment is strong, limiting extensive-margin responses but allowing some intensive-margin adjustment. Microsimulation models like those used by CBO or in academic exercises (e.g., calibrated to Gruber-Saez type taxable income elasticities) typically assign substitution elasticities in the range of 0.15–0.30 for this group. I center my estimate at 0.20, with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45 to account for different model specifications and definitions of the response coefficient.
